# B08: Stakeholder Intelligence

## Section 1: Foundational Profile

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **Name** | Heather Wixson |
| **Title** | Career Coach |
| **Organization** | Landedly |
| **Product/Service** | Landedly is a career services company using a deferred-payment model — candidates receive coaching upfront and pay after landing a job (or at 9 months). ~15 person team, 3 coaches, plus "implementation specialists" who execute on coach recommendations (resume, LinkedIn). |
| **Motivation** | Wants better tooling to manage and track her candidates' progress. Frustrated with current platform (Huntr). Genuinely cares about post-onboarding outcomes — "we're not gonna be able to sit in that interview" — and is looking for solutions that bridge the last-mile gap between prep and placement. |
| **Funding Status** | Unknown — needs enrichment. Landedly is deliberately holding back on promotion while working out internal processes, suggesting early-stage operational maturity. |
| **Key Challenges** | 1) No visibility into candidate progress (struggling with Huntr). 2) Gap between onboarding/coaching and actual placement outcomes. 3) Still ramping up internally — her supervisor was on vacation her first week. 4) Retention and re-engagement for candidates who aren't landing. |
| **Standout Quote** | "You've done all this onboarding, all of this... but we're not gonna be able to sit in that interview." |

## Section 2: What Resonated

### Careerspan's Employer-Side Placement Model
- **Quote:** "Oh, I like that. I didn't realize you guys were moving in that direction."
- **Why it resonated:** She was genuinely surprised and her trailing off signals real-time recalculation of the partnership potential. This directly addresses her core concern — the gap between coaching and placement.
- **Signal/Implication:** Heather sees Careerspan's community-sourced hiring model as solving Landedly's biggest structural weakness. She's likely to champion this internally.

### Careerspan as Interview Prep + Job Fit Tool
- **Quote:** "I always think of Careerspan is really great interview prep, as well as kind of looking at job postings you're thinking of applying for and all kinds of just cool things."
- **Why it resonated:** Unprompted endorsement based on her own experience with the product. Not a response to a pitch — she volunteered this framing.
- **Signal/Implication:** Heather already has a mental model for where Careerspan fits in her coaching workflow. She positions it as a tool for independent candidate work between coaching sessions.

### Vibe Check Feature
- **Quote:** (Active engagement during demo, no objections, leaned into the gap analysis concept)
- **Why it resonated:** The Vibe Check feature directly maps to her coaching workflow — she helps candidates figure out what roles to target and how to present themselves. Having an objective fit score adds structure to conversations she's already having.
- **Signal/Implication:** Vibe Check is the feature most likely to be adopted first by her and her clients.

### ATS-Skipping Direct Placement
- **Quote:** (Strong positive response to "you will go directly to the hiring manager's desk")
- **Why it resonated:** This is the most tangible value prop for her job seekers. Mid-career professionals stuck in ATS black holes is a pain point she sees daily.
- **Signal/Implication:** This is the headline she'll relay to Weston. It transforms Careerspan from "a nice coaching tool" to "a placement engine."

### Warm Introduction Program
- **Quote:** (Immediately asked whether the leads come from the Warmer Jobs partnership — showing she was tracking the business model closely)
- **Why it resonated:** Heather was connecting the dots between Careerspan's lead sources and how the distribution model works in practice. She's thinking like a partner, not just a user.
- **Signal/Implication:** She's analytically engaged with the business model, not just the product. Good sign for a partnership champion.

I think this could be a really good fit." 4) Her previous engagement was strong enough that she returned when she had a new use case.
